title: Badge & Avatar
intro: Two small identity primitives. `wf-badge` is a pill-shaped status label whose color variant must map to a real semantic intent — never decoration. `wf-avatar` is a circular initials-or-image chip with four sizes, a stacked group, and a corner presence dot.

blocks.title: Badge
blocks.body:
`wf-badge` is a fully-rounded (`border-radius: 9999px`) inline pill at 12px / weight 500. Bare, it's the neutral default — gray fill, gray text — for categories, tags, and inactive states. Four color modifiers layer on a tinted background plus a darker text color: `wf-badge-primary` (blue, informational / in-progress), `wf-badge-success` (green, healthy / active / completed), `wf-badge-warning` (amber, pending / attention-needed), and `wf-badge-error` (red, failed / expired / cancelled).

Each color is a status signal, not a paint job — the same rule the rest of the system lives by (`DESIGN.md §3.1`): status colors are reserved for status, no exceptions. Sizes are `wf-badge-sm` and `wf-badge-lg` around the default; the badge also happily holds a count (`12`, `99+`) instead of a word.

blocks.title: Status color variants
blocks.caption: The five intents: bare default (neutral gray), primary (blue/informational), success (green), warning (amber), error (red). The fill is a light tint, the text a darker shade of the same hue.

blocks.title: Badge variants
blocks.intro: One color modifier per badge, layered on the base `wf-badge` class. Size modifiers (`wf-badge-sm`, `wf-badge-lg`) compose with any color.

rows.name: wf-badge
rows.when: Neutral default — categories, tags, inactive or draft states. Gray fill, gray text.

rows.name: wf-badge-primary
rows.when: Informational / in-progress states — In Review, In Progress, Medium priority.

rows.name: wf-badge-success
rows.when: Healthy / completed / positive status — Active, Executed, Approved, Online.

rows.name: wf-badge-warning
rows.when: Pending / attention-needed status — Pending, Pending Signature, High priority.

rows.name: wf-badge-error
rows.when: Hard-fail / negative status — Expired, Cancelled, Failed, Critical.

rows.name: wf-badge-sm
rows.when: Compact contexts — inside dense list rows, card meta, profile chips.

rows.name: wf-badge-lg
rows.when: Standalone emphasis where the badge is the headline, not a footnote.

blocks.title: Dot variants
blocks.caption: `wf-badge-dot` indicators across the same intents, shown bare (top) and paired with a text label via `wf-badge-with-dot` (bottom). Inside a labelled wrapper the dot shrinks from 8px to 6px.

blocks.title: Dot badge variants
blocks.intro: A `wf-badge-dot` is a tiny round status indicator (8px) for presence and at-a-glance status without a pill. Wrap it in `wf-badge-with-dot` to attach a text label — which you almost always should, since the dot alone is color-only.

rows.name: wf-badge-dot
rows.when: Bare neutral dot — gray (`--wf-color-neutral-400`), used for offline / unset.

rows.name: wf-badge-dot-success
rows.when: Green dot for online / healthy presence.

rows.name: wf-badge-dot-warning
rows.when: Amber dot for away / attention.

rows.name: wf-badge-dot-error
rows.when: Red dot for busy / error.

rows.name: wf-badge-dot-primary
rows.when: Blue dot for an informational / active marker.

rows.name: wf-badge-with-dot
rows.when: The accessible pairing — dot + readable label so color isn't the only signal.

blocks.title: Badge color tokens
blocks.intro: Each color variant is a fill/text pair defined at `:root` in `data-display.css`. The default pair references the neutral scale; the four status pairs are literal tinted backgrounds with a darker same-hue text.

blocks.title: Avatar
blocks.body:
`wf-avatar` is a 40px circle that holds either `wf-avatar-initials` (uppercase, weight 600, neutral-gray text on a neutral-gray fill) or an image via `wf-avatar-image` (object-fit: cover). Initials are the fallback when no photo exists — the markup is designed so the same circle works either way.

Four sizes: `wf-avatar-sm` (32px), default (40px), `wf-avatar-lg` (56px), `wf-avatar-xl` (80px) — and the initials font scales with each. Stack several with `wf-avatar-group` (each child gets a 2px white ring and a -12px overlap; the first child resets its margin), and cap the stack with a `wf-avatar-count` chip (`+5`). For presence, wrap the avatar in `wf-avatar-wrapper` and drop a `wf-avatar-status` dot in the bottom-right corner.

blocks.title: Avatar sizes
blocks.caption: Small (32px), Medium (40px / default), Large (56px), X-Large (80px). The `wf-avatar-initials` font scales 12 / 14 / 20 / 28px to match.

blocks.title: Avatar variants
blocks.intro: The base `wf-avatar` is the medium (40px) circle. Size, group, count, and status classes compose onto it.

rows.name: wf-avatar
rows.when: Default 40px circle with initials fallback.

rows.name: wf-avatar-image
rows.when: Photo avatar — image fills and crops to the circle (object-fit: cover).

rows.name: wf-avatar-sm
rows.when: 32px — list rows, activity feeds, inline name chips.

rows.name: wf-avatar-lg
rows.when: 56px — section headers, prominent assignees.

rows.name: wf-avatar-xl
rows.when: 80px — profile cards and hero identity blocks.

rows.name: wf-avatar-group
rows.when: Stack of overlapping avatars (-12px overlap, 2px white ring) for shared assignment.

rows.name: wf-avatar-count
rows.when: Overflow chip closing a group — gray fill, `+N` instead of initials.

blocks.title: Status indicator
blocks.caption: `wf-avatar-wrapper` positions a `wf-avatar-status` dot in the corner: online (green), away (amber), busy (red), offline (gray). The 2px white border keeps the dot legible against any avatar fill.

blocks.title: Presence (status dot)
blocks.intro: Presence is a separate concern from the avatar: wrap the avatar in `wf-avatar-wrapper` (position: relative) so the absolutely-positioned `wf-avatar-status` dot anchors to its bottom-right corner.

rows.name: wf-avatar-status-online
rows.when: Green — user is active. Maps to `--wf-color-success`.

rows.name: wf-avatar-status-away
rows.when: Amber — idle / away. Maps to `--wf-color-warning`.

rows.name: wf-avatar-status-busy
rows.when: Red — do-not-disturb / busy. Maps to `--wf-color-error`.

rows.name: wf-avatar-status-offline
rows.when: Gray — offline / unknown. Maps to `--wf-color-neutral-400`.

blocks.title: Avatar & dot tokens
blocks.intro: Avatar fills come from the neutral scale; status dots reuse the same semantic status colors as the badge so presence reads consistently with the rest of the system.

blocks.title: Canonical usage
blocks.code:
Active Pending Signature Expired Online
JD
JD
JD
AS
+5
blocks.note: The status dot lives in `wf-avatar-wrapper`, not inside `wf-avatar` — the avatar circle has `overflow: hidden`, so a dot placed inside would be clipped.

blocks.title: Color is a signal, not decoration
blocks.intro: These are the system's hard rules (`DESIGN.md §3.1`, §3.3) applied to badges and dots.

items.rule: A status color only appears when there is real status to report.
items.why: Success green means healthy/done; warning amber means pending; error red means failed. Painting a category tag green to make it 'pop' is a §3.1 violation.

items.rule: Categories, tags, and counts stay on the bare neutral `wf-badge`.
items.why: If a tag isn't reporting status, it has no business borrowing a status color — reserve the palette so the colored badges still mean something.

items.rule: Color is never the only signal — pair every status color with its label.
items.why: DESIGN.md §3.3 / §6.3: red alone isn't an error. The badge text or the `wf-badge-with-dot` label is the non-color signal that carries to colorblind and AT users.

items.rule: Don't recolor presence dots for emphasis.
items.why: online/away/busy/offline map 1:1 to success/warning/error/neutral. Reusing those hues for a 'new' or 'featured' marker collides with their reserved meaning.
Error red is reserved for hard-fail status (DESIGN.md §3.1). A premium/marketing label borrowing red trains users to ignore the color where it actually warns.
A product category is neutral, not status — it stays on the bare gray badge. Green here dilutes the meaning of 'healthy/active' everywhere else.
A lone red dot communicates nothing to a colorblind user. §3.3: pair the dot with a text label so the signal isn't color-only.
Warning amber is reserved for attention-needed status (§3.1 'Never used for: highlighting, tip callouts'). Use weight or hierarchy for emphasis, not the status palette.

items.title: Default badge has no modifier class
items.detail: The neutral pill is just `wf-badge` — there is no `wf-badge-default`. The color modifiers (`-primary`, `-success`, `-warning`, `-error`) override the default fill/text pair that ships on the base class.

items.title: Status dot must live outside the avatar circle
items.detail: `wf-avatar` sets `overflow: hidden` so images crop cleanly — a `wf-avatar-status` dot placed inside the circle gets clipped. Put the dot as a sibling inside `wf-avatar-wrapper` (which is `position: relative`); the dot is absolutely positioned to the corner.

items.title: Group children need the group as parent to overlap
items.detail: The -12px overlap, 2px white ring, and first-child margin reset are all scoped to `.wf-avatar-group .wf-avatar`. A bare `wf-avatar` outside the group won't overlap — and `wf-avatar-count` only gets its gray fill inside the group context.

items.title: Dot shrinks inside a labelled wrapper
items.detail: A standalone `wf-badge-dot` is 8px, but `.wf-badge-with-dot .wf-badge-dot` narrows it to 6px so it sits proportionally beside text. Same class, two sizes depending on context.

items.title: Badge color tokens are literal hex, not aliases
items.detail: The four status fills (`#dbeafe`, `#dcfce7`, `#fef3c7`, `#fee2e2`) and their text colors are defined directly in `data-display.css`, not pulled from `tokens.css`. The badge tints are intentionally lighter than the solid `--wf-color-success`/`-warning`/`-error` used for dots — don't expect a colored badge and a status dot of the 'same' intent to be the identical hue.
